title: 第 11 節
en: Think only what the result would be if, in England, the criminal drunkard and the sister of mercy who comes to visit him in his miserable den were asked to give an account of their common Christianity, and you will be less surprised, I believe, at the discrepancies in the reports given by different witnesses of the creed of one and the same African tribe.
Authority of priests.
It might be said that the priests, when consulted on the religious opinions of their people, ought to be 1 Waitz, ' Anthropologie,' ii. 171.

•unimpeachable authorities. But is that so 1 Is it so with us \ We have witnessed ourselves, not many years ago, how one of the most eminent theologians declared that one whose bust now stands with those of Keble and Kingsley in the same chapel of Westminster Abbey, did not believe in the same God as himself! Need we wonder, then, if priests among the Ashantis differ as to the true meaning of their fetishes, and if travellers who have listened to different teachers of religion differ in the accounts which they give to us \ In some parts of Africa, particularly where the influence of Mohammedanism is felt, fetishes and sellers of fetishes are despised. The people who believe in them are called thiedos, or infidels1. In other parts, fetish-worship rules supreme, and priests who manufacture fetishes and live by the sale of them shout very loudly, l Great is Diana of the Ephesians.'
Unwillingness of savages to talk of religion.

Lastly, let us consider that, in order to get at a real understanding of any religion, there must be a wish and a will on both sides. Many savages shrink from questions on religious topics, partly, it may be, from some superstitious fear — partly, it may be, from their helplessness in putting their own unfinished thoughts and sentiments into definite language. Some races are decidedly reticent. Speaking is an effort to them. After ten minutes conversation, they complain of headache2. Others are extremely talka- 1 "Waitz, ii. 200. 'On Different Classes of Priests,' ii. 199.
2 Burchell, 'Reisen in das Innere von Siidafrika/ 1823, p. 71, 281. Schultze, ' Fetischismus/ p. 36. H. Spencer, 'Sociology,' i. p. 94.
IS FETISHISM A PEIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION ? 95 tive, and have an answer to everything, little caring whether what they say is true or not1.

This difficulty is admirably stated by the Rev. B. H. Codrington, in a letter from Norfolk Island, July 3, 1877: 'But the confusion about such matters does not ordinarily lie in the native mind, but pro ceeds from the want of clear communication between the native and European. A native who knows a little English, or one trying to communicate with an Englishman in his native tongue, finds it very much more easy to assent to what the white man suggests, or to use the words that he knows, without perhaps exactly knowing the meaning, than to struggle to convey exactly what he thinks is the true account. Hence visitors receive what they suppose trustworthy information from natives, and then print things which read very absurdly to those who know the truth. Much amusement was caused to-day when I told a Merlav boy that I had just read in a book (Capt. Moresby's on New Guinea) of the idols he had seen in his village, which it was hoped that boy would be able to teach the natives to reject. He had a hand in making them, and they are no more idols than the gurgoyles on your chapel ; yet I have no doubt some native told the naval officers that they were idols, or devils, or something, when he was asked whether they were not, and got much credit for his knowledge of English/ I mentioned in my first Lecture the account of some excellent Benedictine 2 missionaries, who, after 1 Mayer, ' Papua-sprachen,' p. 19.
2 A Benedictine Missionary's account of the natives of Australia and Oceania. From the Italian of Don Rudesindo Salvado (Rome, 96 LECTURE II.

three years spent at their station in Australia, came to the conclusion that the natives did not adore any deity, whether true or false. Yet they found out afterwards that the natives -believed in an omnipotent Being, who had created the world. Suppose they had left their station before having made this dis covery, who would have dared to contradict their statements ?

De Brosses, when he gave his first and fatal account of fetishism, saw none of these difficulties. Whatever he found in the voyages of sailors and traders was welcome to him. He had a theory to defend, and whatever seemed to support it, was sure to be true.

I have entered thus fully into the difficulties inherent in the study of the religions of savage tribes, in order to show how cautious we ought to be before we accept one-sided descriptions of these religions ; still more, before we venture to build on such evidence as is now accessible, far-reaching theories on the nature and origin of religion in general. It will be difficult indeed to eradicate the idea of a universal primeval fetishism from the text books of history. That very theory has become a kind of scientific fetish, though, like most fetishes, it seems to owe its existence to ignorance and super stition.
Only let me not be misunderstood. I do not mean to dispute the fact that fetish-worship is widely prevalent among the negroes of Western Africa and other savage races.
1851), by C. H. E. Carmichael. 'Journal of the Anthropological Institute,' February, 1878.

IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION ? 97 What I cannot bring myself to admit is that any writer on the subject, beginning with De Brosses, has proved, or ever attempted to prove, that what they call fetishism is a primitive form of religion. It may be admitted to be a low form, but that, par ticularly in religion, is very different from a primitive form of religion.
Wide extension of the meaning of fetish.

One of the greatest difficulties we have to en counter in attempting to deal in a truly scientific spirit with the problem of fetishism, is the wide extension that has been given to the meaning of the word fetish.

De Brosses speaks already of fetishes, not only in Africa, but among the Red Indians, the Polynesians, the northern tribes of Asia ; and after his time hardly a single corner of the world has been visited without traces of fetish- worship being discovered. I am the last man to deny to this spirit which sees similarities everywhere, its scientific value and justi fication. It is the comparative spirit which is at work everywhere, and which has achieved the greatest triumphs in modern times. But we must not forget that comparison, in order to be fruitful, must be joined with distinction, otherwise we fall into that dangerous habit of seeing cromlechs wherever there are some upright stones and another laid across, or a dolmen wherever we meet with a stone with a hole in it.

We have heard a great deal lately in Germany, and in England also, of tree-worship and serpentworship. Nothing can be more useful than a wide collection of analogous facts, but their true scientific H 98 LECTURE II.
interest begins only when we can render to ourselves an account of how, beneath their apparent similarity, there often exists the greatest diversity of origin.

It is the same in Comparative Philology. No doubt there is grammar everywhere, even in the languages of the lowest races ; but if we attempt to force our grammatical terminology, our nominatives and accusatives, our actives and passives, our gerunds and supines upon every language, we lose the chief lesson which a comparative study of language is to teach us, and we fail to see how the same object can be realised, and was realised, in a hundred different ways, in a hundred different languages. Here, better than anywhere else, the old Latin saying applies : Si duo dicunt idem, non est idem, ' If two languages say the same thing, it is not the same thing.5 If there is fetish-worship everywhere, the fact is curious, no doubt ; but it gains a really scientific value only if we can account for the fact. How a fetish came to be a fetish, that is the problem which has to be solved, and as soon as we attack fetishism in that spirit, we shall find that, though being appa rently the same everywhere, its antecedents are seldom the same anywhere. There is no fetish with out its antecedents, and it is in these antecedents alone that its true and scientific interest consists.
Antecedents of fetishism.
Let us consider only a few of the more common forms of what has been called fetishism ; and we shall soon see from what different heights and depths its sources spring.

If the bones, or the ashes, or the hair of a departed friend are cherished as relics, if they are kept in safe IS FETISHISM A PRIMITIVE FORM OF RELIGION ? 99 or sacred places, if they are now and then looked at, or even spoken to, by true mourners in their loneliness, all this may be, and has been, called fetish-worship.

Again, if a sword once used by a valiant warrior, if a banner which had led their fathers to victory, if a stick, or let us call it a sceptre, if a calabash, or let us call it a drum, are greeted with respect or en thusiasm by soldiers when going to do battle them selves, all this may be called fetish-worship. If these banners and swords are blessed by priests, or if the spirits of those who had carried them in former years are invoked, as if they were still present, all this may be put down as fetishism. If the defeated soldier breaks his sword across his knees, or tears his colours, or throws his eagles away, he may be said to be punishing his fetish ; nay, Napoleon himself may be called a fetish-worshipper when, pointing to the pyramids, he said to his soldiers, ' From the summit of these monuments forty centuries look down upon you, soldiers ! '
This is a kind of comparison in which similarities are allowed to obscure all differences.

No, we cannot possibly distinguish too much, if we want not only to know, but to understand the ancient customs of savage nations. Sometimes a stock or a stone was worshipped, because it was a forsaken altar, or an ancient place of judgment1 ; sometimes because it marked the place of a great battle or a murder 2, or the burial of a king ; sometimes because it protected the sacred boundaries of clans or families. There are stones from which weapons can be made ; there are stones on which w.eapons can be 1 Paus. i. 28, 5. 2 Ibid. viii. 13, 3; x. 5, 4.

sharpened ; there are stones, like the jade found in Swiss lakes, that must have been brought as heir looms from great distances ; there are meteoric stones fallen from the sky. Are all these simply to be labelled fetishes, because, for very good but very different reasons, they were treated with some kind of reverence by ancient and even by modern people 1 Sometimes the fact that a crude stone is wor shipped as the image of a god may show a higher power of abstraction than the worship paid to the master-works of Phidias ; sometimes the worship paid to a stone slightly resembling the human form may mark a very low stage of religious feeling. If we are satisfied with calling all this and much more simply fetishism, we shall soon be told that the stone on which all the kings of England have been crowned is an old fetish, and that in the coronation of Queen Victoria we ought to recognise a survival of Anglo-Saxon fetishism.

Matters have at last gone so far that people travelling in Africa actually cross-examine the natives whether they believe in fetishes, as if the poor negro or the Hottentot, or the Papua could have any idea of what is meant by such a word ! Native African words for fetish are gri-gri, gru-gru, or ju-ju, all of them possibly the same word1. I must quote at least one story, showing how far superior the examinee may sometimes be to the examiners. 'A negro was worshipping a tree, sup posed to be his fetish, with an offering of food, when 1 "Waltz, ii. p. 175. F. Schultze states that the negroes adopted that word from the Portuguese. Bastian gives enquizi as a name for fetish on the West Coast of Africa ; also mokisso (Bastian, 'St. Salvador,' pp. 254, 81).
zh: 試想，若是在英格蘭，人們要求一名犯罪的醉漢與一位前來他那破敗陋室探訪的慈悲修女，各自陳述他們共同的基督教信仰，其結果將會如何。我相信，屆時你對於不同見證者就同一個非洲部落信條所提出的報告中出現的歧異，便不會感到那麼驚訝了。

祭司的權威，在宗教學的視野中，並非源自其個人，而是來自於他們所宣稱代表的、超越人類理解範疇的「無限者」（the Infinite）。這種權威，往往透過對神聖經典的獨佔解釋權，以及對儀式程序的嚴格掌控來體現。在吠陀（Veda）時期的印度，尤其是《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）的頌詩中，我們可以看到祭司（婆羅門）如何透過精確無誤的咒語與祭祀，維繫著宇宙秩序，並在此過程中鞏固了自身無可撼動的社會地位。他們的權威，與其說是建立在個人德行或知識之上，不如說是奠基於一種被認為是永恆且非人為的、來自「無限者」的啟示。這種現象，在比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的框架下，並非雅利安（Aryan）或閃語族（Semitic）傳統所獨有，而是在人類宗教發展的早期階段，一種普遍存在的結構性特徵。
> 有人或許會說，當祭司被問及其族群的宗教觀點時，他們理應……¹ ¹ 瓦茨，《人類學》，卷二，第171頁。

• 無可指摘的權威。但果真如此嗎？我們自身難道不也是如此？就在不多年前，我們親眼見證，一位最傑出的神學家宣稱，那位如今其半身像與凱布爾（Keble）及金斯利（Kingsley）並列於西敏寺同一禮拜堂的人，所信仰的上帝與他自己並不相同！既然如此，我們又何須驚訝於阿散蒂人（Ashantis）的祭司對其護符（fetishes）的真正含義看法分歧，又何必驚訝於聽從不同宗教導師的旅行者，在他們向我們提供的記述中彼此矛盾？在非洲某些地區，特別是在感受到伊斯蘭教影響的地方，護符及其販售者備受鄙視。信奉護符的人被稱為「thiedos」，即異教徒。在其他地區，護符崇拜則至高無上，那些製造護符並靠販售維生的祭司高聲呼喊：「以弗所的亞底米（Diana）何等偉大！」
野蠻人對於談論宗教一事，往往表現出極不情願的態度。

最後，我們必須考量：要真正理解任何宗教，雙方都必須有意願與決心。許多未開化民族對宗教議題的提問避之唯恐不及——部分原因或許出於某種迷信的恐懼，部分原因則可能是他們難以將自身未臻成熟的思想與情感化為明確的語言。有些種族顯然生性緘默。對他們而言，開口說話本身就是一種負擔。交談十分鐘後，他們便會抱怨頭痛¹。另一些種族則極度健談²。 ¹ Waitz, ii. 200. ² '論祭司的不同階層,' ii. 199.
2  Burchell，《南非洲內陸旅行記》（Reisen in das Innere von Südafrika），1823 年，第 71、281 頁。Schultze，《物神崇拜》（Fetischismus），第 36 頁。H. Spencer，《社會學》（Sociology），第一卷，第 94 頁。
95 消極的，且對萬事萬物都有答案，卻毫不在意自己所說的是否為真¹。

此一困難，在 B. H. 科德林頓牧師（Rev. B. H. Codrington）於 1877 年 7 月 3 日自諾福克島（Norfolk Island）寄出的一封信中，得到了極佳的陳述：「然而，關於這類事情的混亂，通常並非源於原住民的心智，而是出自原住民與歐洲人之間缺乏清晰的溝通。一個略懂英語的原住民，或是一個試圖用自己的母語與英國人溝通的人，會發現：順從白人的暗示，或使用他所知道的詞彙（即使可能不完全確知其意），遠比費力去精確傳達他認為的真實說法要容易得多。因此，訪客從原住民那裡接收到他們以為可信的資訊，然後印行出來，結果在知情者看來荒謬至極。今天，當我告訴一個梅拉夫（Merlav）男孩，我剛在一本書（莫爾斯比船長關於新幾內亞的著作）中讀到他村裡所見的偶像，並希望這男孩能教導原住民摒棄那些偶像時，引起了許多笑聲。那些東西是他親手參與製作的，它們與你們教堂上的滴水獸（gargoyles）一樣，根本不是偶像；然而，我毫不懷疑，當某個原住民被海軍軍官問及那些東西是否為偶像時，他回答『是』，並因其英語能力而大受讚賞，於是那些東西就成了偶像、魔鬼或諸如此類的東西。」我在第一講中曾提及一些優秀的本篤會（Benedictine）傳教士的記述¹，他們在……¹ 邁爾（Mayer），〈巴布亞諸語言〉（Papua-sprachen），第 19 頁。
一位本篤會傳教士對澳洲與大洋洲原住民的記述。譯自唐‧魯德辛多‧薩爾瓦多（Don Rudesindo Salvado）的義大利文著作（羅馬，96頁）。
在澳洲駐地度過三年後，他們得出結論：當地原住民並不崇拜任何神祇，無論是真神還是假神。然而，他們後來發現，這些原住民其實信仰一位全能的存有（omnipotent Being），祂創造了世界。假設他們在做出這項發現之前就離開了駐地，又有誰敢於反駁他們的說法呢？
德‧布霍斯（De Brosses）在首次提出其關於物神崇拜（fetishism）的致命論述時，並未察覺這些困難。凡是他在水手與商人的航海記述中所發現的，他都欣然接納。他有一套理論需要捍衛，而任何看似支持該理論的內容，都必定被視為真理。
我之所以如此詳盡地探討原始部落宗教研究中所固有的困難，是為了說明我們在接納這些宗教的片面描述之前，應當何等審慎；更何況，在我們膽敢依據目前可及的這類證據，建構關於宗教本質與起源的宏大理論之前，尤須謹慎。要從歷史教科書中根除「普遍原始拜物教」這個觀念，確實困難重重。該理論本身已成為一種科學上的拜物教，儘管——正如大多數拜物教一般——它的存在似乎應歸因於無知與迷信。
請勿誤解我的意思。我並非否認拜物崇拜（fetish-worship）廣泛存在於西非黑人及其他野蠻種族之間的事實。
> 「宗教學（the science of religion）……在比較的基礎上，對世界各大宗教進行歷史性的研究。」——C. H. E. 卡邁克爾（C. H. E. Carmichael），1851 年。引自《人類學研究所期刊》（Journal of the Anthropological Institute），1878 年 2 月。
我無法接受的是，從德‧布羅斯（De Brosses）開始的任何相關論述者，都未曾證明、甚至未曾試圖證明，他們所謂的物神崇拜（fetishism）是宗教的原始形式。或許可以承認它是一種低等形式，但這——尤其在宗教領域——與宗教的原始形式截然不同。
在嘗試以真正科學的精神處理物神崇拜（fetishism）問題時，我們所面臨的最大困難之一，便是「物神」（fetish）一詞的意義已被過度擴展。

德布羅塞斯早已提及物神崇拜，不僅在非洲，也在紅印第安人、玻里尼西亞人、亞洲北部部落中；在他之後，世上幾乎沒有一個角落被探訪而不發現物神崇拜的痕跡。我絕非否認這種處處察見相似性的精神所具有的科學價值與正當性。這種比較精神無所不在，並在現代取得了最偉大的成就。但我們不可忘記，比較若要富有成果，就必須與區別相結合；否則我們便會陷入那種危險的習慣——只要見到幾塊豎立的石頭，上面橫放另一塊，就認為是石棚（cromlech）；或者只要遇到一塊有洞的石頭，就以為是石桌墳（dolmen）。
我們近來在德國，乃至英格蘭，也聽聞了許多關於樹木崇拜與蛇類崇拜的討論。廣泛蒐集類比事實固然極具價值，然而其真正的科學意義——
唯有當我們能向自己說明，在表面相似性之下，往往存在著起源上最巨大的歧異時，真正的趣味才開始。

在我們能夠充分理解物神崇拜（fetishism）之前，必須先追溯其更早的根源。語言科學（the science of language）教導我們，人類心靈在最初命名與分類現象時，便已不自覺地為後來的宗教觀念鋪路。當原始人試圖以有限的詞彙捕捉無限者（the Infinite）時，每一個名稱都帶有隱喻的性質，而這些隱喻隨著時間流逝，其原始意義逐漸模糊，最終凝固為獨立的存在實體。 這種過程在雅利安（Aryan）與閃語族（Semitic）的早期文獻中尤為明顯。以吠陀（Veda）為例，梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中的諸神如特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva），最初不過是自然現象的人格化名稱，但後代卻將這些名稱視為獨立的神祇。穆勒（Müller）指出，這種「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）正是多神教（polytheism）與物神崇拜的共同溫床。 值得注意的是，物神崇拜並非宗教演化的起點，而是某種更古老直覺的退化形式。在單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的階段，崇拜者雖暫時專注於單一神祇，卻仍保留對其他神明的承認；然而，當這種專註失去其哲學基礎，淪為對具體物件的盲目依附時，物神崇拜便應運而生。圖蘭語族（Turanian）的某些部落便展現了這種從抽象神聖概念墮落為具體物神崇拜的典型軌跡。
讓我們僅考量所謂「拜物教」的幾種較常見形式；我們很快便會發現，其源頭是從何等不同的高處與深處湧現而出。
> 若逝去友人的骨骸、骨灰或毛髮被當作聖物珍藏，若被保存在安全或神聖之處，若孤獨的真摯哀悼者時不時凝視它們，甚至對它們說話——這一切可能被稱為，也確實曾被稱為，物神崇拜。

弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒 再者，若一名勇士曾用過的劍、一面曾引領其父輩走向勝利的旗幟、一根棍杖（或不妨稱之為權杖）、一隻葫蘆（或不妨稱之為鼓），在士兵們即將親赴戰場之際，受到他們的尊敬或熱情歡呼，這一切皆可稱為物神崇拜。若這些旗幟與刀劍經祭司祝聖，或若昔日持用這些器物者的靈魂被召喚，彷彿他們仍在場，這一切亦可歸為物神崇拜。若戰敗的士兵將自己的劍橫跨膝上折斷、撕裂軍旗、或拋棄鷹徽，便可說他是在懲罰自己的物神；甚至拿破崙本人，當他手指金字塔對士兵說：「士兵們！從這些紀念碑的頂端，四十個世紀正俯視著你們。」亦可被稱為物神崇拜者。
這是一種比較方式，其中相似性被允許掩蓋所有差異。

不，我們絕不可能過度區分，如果我們不僅要認識，更要理解野蠻民族的古老習俗。有時，一根木樁或一塊石頭之所以受到崇拜，是因為它是一座被遺棄的祭壇，或是一個古老的審判場所¹；有時，是因為它標誌著一場大戰或謀殺的地點²，或是一位國王的埋葬之處；有時，是因為它保護了氏族或家族的神聖邊界。有些石頭可以製成武器；有些石頭則可以磨利武器。 ¹ 保薩尼亞斯《希臘誌》卷一，28，5。 ² 同上，卷八，13，3；卷十，5，4。

尖銳化了；有些石頭，例如瑞士湖泊中發現的玉，必定是作為傳家寶從遠方帶來的；還有從天而降的隕石。難道這些東西僅僅因為古代甚至現代的人們出於非常合理但截然不同的理由而對它們懷有某種敬意，就該一律被貼上「物神」（fetishes）的標籤嗎？¹ 有時，一塊未經雕琢的石頭被當作神像崇拜，可能展現出比崇拜菲迪亞斯（Phidias）傑作更高的抽象能力；有時，對一塊略似人形的石頭的崇拜，則可能標誌著宗教情感的極低階段。如果我們滿足於將這一切以及更多現象簡單地稱為「物神崇拜」（fetishism），那麼很快就會有人告訴我們，所有英格蘭國王加冕時所用的那塊石頭就是一個古老的物神，而在維多利亞女王的加冕典禮中，我們應當辨識出一種盎格魯–撒克遜物神崇拜的殘存。

事情終究發展到這般地步：在非洲旅行的人們竟會盤問當地土著是否信仰「物神」（fetish），彷彿那可憐的黑人、霍屯督人或巴布亞人，能明白這個詞究竟意指何物！非洲土著用來指稱物神的詞彙是 gri-gri、gru-gru 或 ju-ju，這些詞很可能同源¹。我至少必須引用一則故事，藉此說明受盤問者有時遠比盤問者高明得多：「一名黑人正以食物供奉一棵被視為其物神的樹木，此時……」 ¹ 瓦爾茨（Waitz）卷二，第175頁。F. 舒爾策（F. Schultze）指出，黑人從葡萄牙人那裡借用了這個詞。巴斯蒂安（Bastian）則記錄 enquizi 為西非海岸對物神的稱呼；另有 mokisso（巴斯蒂安，《聖薩爾瓦多》，第254、81頁）。
